summary: Gruber examines a recently unearthed internal Nokia slide deck from January 2007, created the day after Apple unveiled the original iPhone. He argues the Nokia team deserves credit for largely recognizing the iPhone's significance — especially its revolutionary touch UI and carrier relationship — while noting their blind spots around third-party software and the mobile web. The deck's most prescient recommendation was that Nokia needed a Chief UI architect and a wholesale reinvention of its design priorities. Gruber frames the story as a cautionary tale about institutional inertia: Nokia's analysts understood the threat intellectually but the company couldn't act on it because its entire business model depended on the old paradigm.
key_points: Nokia's internal team recognized the iPhone's touch UI as potentially industry-changing, unlike competitors like Ballmer and BlackBerry who dismissed the lack of a hardware keyboard
The deck's biggest miss was treating the absence of Java ME support as a weakness, when in fact abandoning the existing mobile software ecosystem was a strategic advantage
Nokia's minimal mention of web browsing revealed a pre-iPhone mindset where mobile devices were platforms for carrier software, not Internet-first devices
The most prescient recommendation — hiring a Chief UI architect and reinventing Nokia's design culture — went unheeded, illustrating institutional inability to change
The Upton Sinclair adage captures the core lesson: organizations struggle to internalize truths that threaten their existing business models
money_quotes: Most of the existing smartphone makers simply could not believe the iPhone's touchscreen keyboard would work.
But all of that software sucked, big time. Sucked to develop, sucked to distribute, sucked to install, sucked to use. Not supporting it was a huge win for the iPhone.
This speaks to Nokia's pre-iPhone mindset that mobile platforms were not really Internet-first devices but instead were mostly for running shitty software from carriers.
It's so easy to get blinded by the way things currently work and to assume they'll keep working that way.

I'd translate that as the presentation team imploring Nokia's leadership to recognize that Apple's primary priority was creating and delivering a great experience, both hardware and software, and Nokia had no such institutional value placed on design quality.
It is difficult to get a man to understand something, when his salary depends on his not understanding it.
key_insight: Nokia's analysts correctly identified the iPhone as a paradigm shift but the company couldn't act on that insight because its entire institutional structure depended on the old paradigm continuing.
